text:
Polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) are widely available in the environment and have been confirmed to exhibit toxicities such as carcinogenicity. As semi-volatile species, PAHs are present in the atmosphere in both gas and particle phases and undergo multiple processes including physical and chemical conversions that affect their atmospheric lifetime and potential for long-range transport (LRT). This review presents an exhaustive summary of the recent scientific studies concerning the reactivity of PAHs with key atmospheric oxidants (i.e., hydroxyl (OH) radicals, nitrate (NO₃) radicals, and ozone (O₃)).

Summary of kinetics (continued) Most gas phase 2–3 ring PAHs are reacted with OH radicals during the day while the role of NO3 radicals is significant during the night. While gas phase rate coefficients are available for most of the low molecular weight PAHs, there is a large number of uncerntainties in rate coefficients for 4-ring PAHs. A detailed description of the complexity associated with heterogeneous chemistry for particle bound PAHs is given. It is shown that the carbon or mineral composition of aerosols strongly affects the heterogeneous reactivity of particle bound PAHs. These "shields" tend to stabilise the PAH structure against oxidation and increase the life time of PAHs on aerosols.

A critical review on Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs) sources, processes, Fate and Transformation 1. Introduction; 2. Formation of oxygenated and nitrated derivatives (oxy- and nitro-PAHs); 3. Comparison of laboratory kinetic data and environmental measurements: Primary vs. secondary atmospheric PAH formation; 4. Sinks of PAHs and associated global transport modeling; 5. Uncertainties and needed research in the atmospheric science of PAHs, focusing on: a) atmospheric fate of 4-ring PAHs and associated environmental impact, and b) evaluation of heterogeneous PAH reactions under ambient conditions.
headline: Fully Human Written
prediction: We believe that this document is fully human-written
